sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 52, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I notice something important: the blue square at (1,1) is now missing from the segmentation! Previously it was:
- b: boundary=[[1, 1], [1, 3], [3, 3], [3, 1]], pixels=8
But now I see:
- b: boundary=[[1, 5], [1, 7], [3, 7], [3, 5]], pixels=8 (this was previously light gray 'w', now it's blue 'b'!)
- b: boundary=[[5, 5], [5, 7]], pixels=3 (this was previously at [[5, 1], [5, 3]], now it's at [[5, 5], [5, 7]])
So the blue square and blue line have moved from col 1-3 to col 5-7! And the light gray square at (1,5) is now blue.
This is a significant change. The blue square/line seems to track the player's position:
- Initially at col 1-3 (leftmost)
- After SPACE, moved to col 5-7
Wait, let me check the initial state. In the initial frame:
- b: boundary=[[1, 1], [1, 3], [3, 3], [3, 1]], pixels=8 (blue square)
- w: boundary=[[1, 5], [1, 7], [3, 7], [3, 5]], pixels=9 (light gray square)
- b: boundary=[[5, 1], [5, 3]], pixels=3 (blue line)
Now after SPACE:
- b: boundary=[[1, 5], [1, 7], [3, 7], [3, 5]], pixels=8 (blue square at col 5-7)
- b: boundary=[[5, 5], [5, 7]], pixels=3 (blue line at col 5-7)
So the blue square and line moved from col 1-3 to col 5-7, and the light gray square at (1,5) is now blue.
This suggests the blue square/line is a "marker" that moves to track something. The light gray square at (1,5) was replaced by the blue square.
Looking at the player positions:
- Initial player: (8,14)-(12,18)
- After SPACE: (8,14)-(12,18) (same)
- Blue square: moved from (1,1)-(3,3) to (1,5)-(3,5)
The blue square moved 4 columns to the right. The player didn't move. So the blue square doesn't track the player.
Maybe the blue square tracks the "progress" through the level. Each SPACE advances the blue square by some amount.
Let me check the bottom bar:
- Initially: b: [[63, 0], [63, 63]], pixels=64 (all blue)
- Now: b: [[63, 0], [63, 38]], pixels=39; w: [[63, 39], [63, 63]], pixels=25
The bottom bar has 39 blue + 25 light gray = 64 pixels. The blue part decreased from 64 to 39, and the light gray part is 25.
So the bottom bar shows remaining steps (blue) and completed steps (light gray). The player has used 25 steps out of 64.
Wait, but the player has used 51 actions so far. So the bottom bar doesn't track the number of actions.
Maybe the bottom bar tracks the number of SPACE actions or some other metric.
Let me think about this differently. The blue square at (1,1) and the light gray square at (1,5) are two separate markers. The blue square moved to the light gray square's position, suggesting they're part of a progress indicator.
Looking at the image, I see:
- Blue square at (1,5)-(3,7)
- Light gray square at (1,1)-(3,3) (was blue before)
- Blue line at (5,5)-(5,7) (was at (5,1)-(5,3) before)
So the blue square and line moved from left to right, and the light gray square is now at the left position.
This suggests a "progress bar" at the top: the blue square moves from left to right, and the light gray square follows behind.
Let me check if there are more positions for the blue square to move to.
Actually, looking at the image, I see:
- Light gray square at (1,1)-(3,3)
- Blue square at (1,5)-(3,7)
- Blue line at (5,5)-(5,7)
The blue square is at col 5-7, and the light gray square is at col 1-3. There's a gap of 2 columns between them.
Maybe the blue square moves to the right each time SPACE is pressed, and the light gray square follows.
Let me try pressing SPACE again and see if the blue square moves further right.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE again to see if the blue square moves
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
